subject: English

sections.heading: ⚔️ Quest Begins: Meet Prabhat, the Win-Obsessed Warrior
sections.body: Our young hero Prabhat loved winning more than anything in the world. Losing made him feel so bad that he would simply refuse to play a game if he thought he might lose — even a simple game of marbles! Imagine if you only played cricket when you were 100% sure your team would win. That's exactly how Prabhat lived. His fear of losing was like a dragon blocking his path to true enjoyment.
keyPoints: Prabhat only played games he was confident of winning
He was very good at badminton and loved that power
His fear of losing stopped him from having real fun

sections.heading: 🐉 The Dragon Appears: Surya Enters the Realm
sections.body: A new student named Surya joined the school and he was a fantastic badminton player. When they played against each other, Surya was relaxed, joking, and smiling — like someone enjoying a Holi festival, not fighting a battle! Surya was winning point after point, and Prabhat got so scared of losing that he did something terrible — he cheated and changed the scoreboard when Surya wasn't looking. He won, but it felt hollow, like eating a ladoo made of mud.
keyPoints: Surya played with joy, not pressure — true sporting spirit
Prabhat cheated to win because he couldn't face defeat
Even after winning by cheating, Prabhat felt unhappy inside

sections.heading: 🔮 The Magic Spell: Prabhat Watches and Learns
sections.body: That night, Prabhat couldn't sleep. Something felt wrong. The next day he saw Surya playing basketball — and Surya was terrible at it! He couldn't score for ten whole minutes. But Surya was laughing and enjoying every moment, like a child playing with pani-puri no matter how messy it gets. Prabhat kept watching Surya for many days and slowly a spell of wisdom touched his heart. He realised you don't need a scoreboard to enjoy a game. The real magic is in the playing, not just the winning.
keyPoints: Surya enjoyed games even when he was bad at them
Prabhat observed that joy comes from playing, not from winning
True mastery means enjoying every moment of the game

sections.heading: 🏆 Power of Sporting Spirit: The Change of Heart
sections.body: Slowly, Prabhat's heart changed — like a hero completing his quest! He started playing hide and seek, joking during badminton, and enjoying even the losses. His friends noticed and said, 'Playing with Prabhat is great fun — he has real sporting spirit!' Sporting spirit means being fair, respecting your opponent, playing by the rules, and enjoying the game whether you win or lose. Think of it like IPL players shaking hands after a match — that's true sportsmanship.
keyPoints: Sporting spirit means being fair and enjoying the game honestly
Prabhat transformed from a cheat to a true sport — his real quest was complete
Cheating may give a false win but never brings real happiness

sections.heading: ✍️ Wizard's Grammar Scroll: Past Perfect Tense
sections.body: The chapter also teaches us a grammar spell called the Past Perfect tense. When two things happened in the past, the thing that happened first uses 'had + verb'. For example: 'Prabhat had already practised before the match started.' It's like saying — I had eaten my dal-rice before Amma called me. The eating happened first, so it uses 'had eaten'. This spell helps us show which action was older.
keyPoints: Past Perfect = had + past participle (e.g., had played, had won)
Use it when showing which past action happened first
Example: 'Surya had played basketball before joining the badminton team'

summary: In this quest, we follow Prabhat — a boy so afraid of losing that he only played games he could win, and even cheated against the skilled new student Surya to avoid defeat. But Surya, a true wizard of sporting spirit, played every game with joy and laughter, whether he won or lost. Watching Surya's attitude every day worked like a magic spell on Prabhat, and his heart slowly changed. He learned that real mastery in games is not about always winning — it's about playing with honesty, enjoying every moment, and respecting your opponent. The chapter also teaches us the grammar power of Past Perfect tense, showing how to talk about two past actions in the right order.
